D9 BiologyL3 ApplyReference

Cat Tapetum Lucidum — The Second-Chance Reflector

behind the cat retina sits a mirrored layer of guanine crystals; light that missed the photoreceptors on the first pass bounces back through for another chance — doubles low-light sensitivity, costs spatial acuity

The tapetum lucidum is a multilayer reflector behind the retina (carnivores, ungulates). Photons that pass through the photoreceptors without absorption bounce off the mirror and traverse the retina a second time, gaining a second chance to be detected. Sensitivity doubles; acuity suffers because the reflected light scatters slightly. Cats see well at 1/6 the light needed by humans. The famous "eye glow" in headlights is tapetum reflection.

Drag light level. Cat sensitivity stays useful far below human threshold.
